name: Anillo Verde Ciclista – complete loop from Casa de Campo
name_original: Anillo Verde Ciclista – vuelta completa desde Casa de Campo
region_intro: No traffic touring cycling routes in Casa De Campo are primarily found within a large urban park in Madrid, Spain, characterized by extensive woodlands and open meadows. The terrain features rolling hills and varied surfaces, including both paved and unpaved sections suitable for touring bicycles. A central feature is the artificial Casa de Campo Lake, around which cyclists can ride. The park offers a natural escape within an urban environment, with motor vehicle access restricted in most areas. Best no traffic touring cycling routes in Casa De Campo The most popular no traffic touring cycling route is Casa de Campo – circular from Metro Batán, a 10.6 miles (17.1 km) trail that takes 1 hour 6 minutes to complete. This route leads through the park's woodlands and open spaces. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Cycling Ring Bridge – Casa de Campo Park loop from Batán, an easy 10.5 miles (16.9 km) path. This trail offers varied scenery within the park, including views of the Cycling Ring Bridge. Local touring cyclists also love the Casa de Campo Park – Casa de Campo Lake loop from Casa de Campo, a 8.1 miles (13.0 km) trail leading through the park and around the artificial lake, often completed in about 52 minutes. Touring cycling in Casa De Campo is defined by extensive woodlands, open meadows, and the Casa de Campo Lake. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy, family-friendly trails to more advanced sections with rolling hills. The routes in Casa De Campo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from more than 2,600 reviews. More than 21,000 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Casa De Campo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many traffic-free touring cycling routes are available in Casa de Campo?
A: Casa de Campo offers a wide selection of traffic-free touring cycling routes. There are over 80 routes available, with a good mix of easy, moderate, and difficult options to suit various skill levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the touring cycling routes in Casa de Campo?
A: The routes in Casa de Campo feature varied terrain, primarily consisting of rolling hills and a mix of both paved and unpaved sections. This allows for diverse cycling experiences, from easier paths to more challenging sections through woodlands and open meadows.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Casa de Campo?
A: Yes, Casa de Campo has several excellent circular touring cycling routes. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e962569514/casa-de-campo-circular-from-metro-batan" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casa de Campo – circular from Metro Batán&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through the park's diverse landscapes. Another great choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38286157" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casa de Campo Park – Casa de Campo Lake loop from Casa de Campo&lt;/a&gt;, offering scenic views around the lake.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or natural features to see along the routes?
A: Cyclists can enjoy several significant landmarks and natural features. The artificial &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/664165" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casa de Campo Lake&lt;/a&gt; is a central attraction, perfect for a scenic ride around its northern perimeter. You can also challenge yourself with the climb up &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2751673" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro Garabitas&lt;/a&gt;, a well-known spot among Madrid's cyclists, or discover the ancient &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5461701" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Plátano Gordo (The Fat Plane Tree)&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there family-friendly touring cycling routes in Casa de Campo?
A: Absolutely! Many routes in Casa de Campo are suitable for families. The park's restricted motor vehicle access makes it a safer and more enjoyable environment for cyclists of all ages. Routes around the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/664165" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casa de Campo Lake&lt;/a&gt; are particularly popular for their flat sections and scenic views.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Casa de Campo?
A: Spring (April-May) and Autumn (September-October) are generally considered the best times for touring cycling in Casa de Campo. The weather is typically mild and pleasant, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, sometimes wetter, conditions of winter. This allows for comfortable rides through the park's extensive woodlands.

Q: How accessible is Casa de Campo by public transport for cyclists?
A: Casa de Campo is very well connected by public transport, making it easy for cyclists to access. Several metro stations, such as Batán and Lago, are located right at the park's edge, providing convenient starting points for many routes. This allows you to reach the park without needing to cycle through city traffic.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about Casa de Campo?
A: The komoot community highly rates touring cycling in Casa de Campo, with an average score of 4.4 stars. Reviewers often praise the extensive network of traffic-free paths, the natural escape provided by its woodlands, and the beautiful views, especially around the lake. The ease of access from Madrid's city center is also frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any routes that offer a more challenging touring cycling experience?
A: For those seeking a more challenging ride, Casa de Campo has options with greater elevation gain and longer distances. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38283640" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Boadilla Forest – Boadilla del Monte Holm Oak Grove loop from Prado de la Vega&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult route that provides a substantial workout. Additionally, the climb to &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2751673" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro Garabitas&lt;/a&gt; is a popular spot for cyclists looking for a consistent gradient.

Q: Can I find cafes or places to eat near the cycling routes?
A: Yes, there are several options for refreshments within and around Casa de Campo. The area around the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/664165" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casa de Campo Lake&lt;/a&gt; is particularly well-equipped with restaurants and cafes where you can take a break and enjoy the views. You'll also find facilities like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1510881" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casa de Vacas Fountain&lt;/a&gt; for water refills.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners?
A: Beginners will find plenty of easy touring cycling routes in Casa de Campo. Many paths are relatively flat and well-maintained, perfect for a relaxed ride. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31590380" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casa de Campo Park – Casa de Campo de Madrid loop from Lago&lt;/a&gt; is a shorter, easy option that allows you to enjoy the park's natural beauty without significant climbs.
tips: It is a very large park and has many beautiful corners to appreciate. There are bike paths, a lake where you can go canoeing and other activities, and a botanical garden area.

What we know as Linear Park actually consists of three large sections. Section 1 is the one that is urbanized and maintained by the Madrid City Council.
It includes a large urban park as well as several leisure and sports facilities, among which "La Caja Mágica" stands out.
This section goes from the Nudo Sur of the M-30 to the Nudo Supersur of the M-40, and is divided between the districts of Usera and Puente de Vallecas.
It is ideal to go with children, given its low elevation. Many paved tracks and good places to stop and rest.
It is part of the Madrid cycling ring.
More at https://bigjoe.es/ruta-por-el-anillo-verde-ciclista-de-madrid/

When I built the Green Belt, the Wanda was still known as the La Peineta stadium. Its real name is Estadio Metropolitano, although it is called Wanda for advertising reasons.
It is now the temple of football for Atlético de Madrid fans (since September 16, 2017) and a spectacular stadium, worth visiting if you pass through the area.

The San Fernando bridge is located in the northwestern part of the municipal term of Madrid. It stands on the Manzanares River,1 in an area near the Monte de El Pardo and the Hippodrome de la Zarzuela, next to the A-6 and the Puerta de Hierro junction.

Philip V ordered a road to be built from the capital to the Royal Site of El Pardo, of which this bridge would form a part. It was built in 1749, during the reign of Fernando VI. Its design was entrusted to the architect Jaime Bort y Meliá. The statues were made by the sculptor Vicente Bort, brother of the architect.

https://bigjoe.es

This artificial pond was commissioned by Felipe II in the 16th century to complete the landscape of the royal site of the Casa de Campo. This urban park is today a refuge in the city for lovers of the outdoors who visit this forest mass on foot and by bicycle.

The lake has several jetties for recreational and sports activities and has hosted several world-class triathlon events. Around it there is a rosary of restaurants with their very popular terraces among the people of Madrid.

This urban park is a former royal hunting ground ceded to the people of Madrid in 1931. Today it is one of the main lungs of the city and a refuge for citizens looking for a bit of recreation and sports in the open air in the middle of the city. Cycling and hiking lovers are the most regulars.

Casa de Campo is the largest park in Madrid and is the habitat of many species of birds, as well as foxes, squirrels and wild boars.
